title: Pricing + usage review with Acme AI
preview: Acme AI flagged a discrepancy between Feb invoice preview and their internal counts/usage exports (invoice billed tokens higher). Redwood explained the current Hosted API metering semantics (billed_tokens derived from prompt+completion tokens, model tokenizer-specific; streaming bills for tokens actually generated; ...
text: Acme AI flagged a discrepancy between Feb invoice preview and their internal counts/usage exports (invoice billed tokens higher). Redwood explained the current Hosted API metering semantics (billed_tokens derived from prompt+completion tokens, model tokenizer-specific; streaming bills for tokens actually generated; retries/fallback should not double-bill but there is a known edge case under certain server retry paths). Nadia confirmed they are rolling out improved dedupe/idempotency and cache_hit attribution fields, plus a "shadow billing compare" to validate changes before they affect invoices. The group agreed on a standard daily export schema (UTC) including request_id, idempotency_key, model, region, raw vs billed tokens, cache_hit fields, retry/fallback indicators, and status so Acme can reconcile per-request. Redwood to run a targeted reconciliation for 2026-02-01 through 2026-0...

Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx

Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Dev Patel
Acme AI: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Miguel Santos, Hannah Wu

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed the Jan 10 Dedicated incident impacting Acme AI (elevated 5xx, latency, and intermittent 429s during a burst window).
- Redwood walked through timeline, mitigations (admission control changes, autoscale ceiling increase, capacity rebalance), and likely root cause (insufficient headroom + routing behavior under saturation).
- Customer feedback focused on earlier warning, clearer status updates, and what remediation SLAs mean for Dedicated.
- Agreed on follow-ups: share PIR, confirm classification, align on comms templates and headroom planning.

title: Acme AI - incident review + diagnostics gaps (deploy/config/saturation)
preview: Acme AI walked Redwood through the 3/10 incident impact (p95 latency spike + intermittent 503s on a subset of routes) and the biggest diagnostic gaps: (1) no easy “what deployed recently” context tied to the incident thread, (2) config/routing changes were hard to confirm quickly and safely (feature flags, model pin...
text: Acme AI walked Redwood through the 3/10 incident impact (p95 latency spike + intermittent 503s on a subset of routes) and the biggest diagnostic gaps: (1) no easy “what deployed recently” context tied to the incident thread, (2) config/routing changes were hard to confirm quickly and safely (feature flags, model pins, quota/rate-limit) without manually paging multiple teams, and (3) saturation signals were inconsistent (GPU util looked fine, but queue depth + KV cache pressure were elevated). Redwood shared planned Incident Bot v2 context cards (recent deploys, tenant-safe config diffs with redaction, saturation signals + deep links), plus constraints around permissioning/audit logging and query budgets. Agreed on a short beta for Acme’s on-call runbooks and which dashboards/links would be most useful.

- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.
